title: Mathematics in machine learning
subject: Maths
transcript:
[0s-10s] How does a machine learning model determine whether an image contains a dog or a cat? Computers cannot process images, texts,
[10s-20s] audio or videos directly like humans. Instead, we need to translate these inputs into a language they can understand, mathematics.
[20s-30s] This translation is made possible by the foundations of linear algebra. Linear algebra provides the framework to represent, manipulate
[30s-40s] and transform data in a form suitable for computation. For example, instead of reading an image directly, linear
[40s-50s] algebra allows us to express it as numerical matrix in high dimensional space. Within this high dimensional space, objects and data types
[50s-60s] can be defined and their relationships and patterns can be measured, compared and learned. The same principles applies
[60s-70s] to all type of data, audios, videos, text or structured data sets. Linear algebra helps convert data into mathematical
[70s-80s] objects through vectorization. In this process, raw data is expressed as objects such as scalar, vector,
[80s-90s] matrix or tensors format that a computer can process and reason about. There are four fundamental data types that form the foundations of linear algebra.
[90s-100s] Scalars, vector,
[100s-110s] matrix and tensor.
[110s-120s] A scalar is the simplest unit, a single number representing a magnitude or quantity. It can be thought of as a single point
[120s-130s] in space such as the number 5 or the float 2.6 or a non terminating decimal like PI. A vector extends
[130s-140s] this idea into one dimensional sequence or list of numbers such as 2, 3, 4 and a third data type is a
[140s-150s] matrix, a two dimensional structure composed of rows and columns. They are capable of representing linear transformations,
[150s-160s] relationships or entire data sets. For instance, an image can be represented as a matrix where each entry correspond
[160s-170s] to a pixel, grayscale or color intensity. A tensor generalizes this idea in even higher dimensional space that's greater
[170s-180s] than 3D. Tensors are also multi dimensional arrays used to represent data in TensorFlow. After transforming or vectorizing raw data into
[180s-190s] linear algebraic objects, we can manipulate them mathematically and prepare them for training in common frameworks such as Pytorch,
[190s-200s] Keras or tensorflow. Vector operations are optimized for performance and can be computed in massive scale. For instance,
[200s-210s] text can be tokenized and represented as a vector or numbers that captures its semantic meaning.
[210s-220s] We can compare how similar two sentences are by calculating distance between their respective vectors or embeddings. They can be measured by a
[220s-230s] number of distance metrics such as the Euclidean distance or cosine similarity. The Euclidean distance.
[230s-240s] Is a numeric measure between two vectors that computes the point wise similarity
[240s-250s] between each dimension and is summed across all dimensions. For example, the Euclidean distance between the vector A and B.
[250s-260s] This is the symbol for vectors is calculated by taking the point wise similarity between the two vectors. So the first
[260s-270s] point is B1 take the similarity of the first point in A plus the second dimension or the
[270s-280s] second point in B. We sum that across all dimensions all the way to bi
[280s-290s] AI squared and we take the square root of that. The output is an unbounded scalar, indicating the
[290s-300s] magnitude of their similarity. The cosine similarity, on the other hand, measures the angle between
[300s-310s] the two vectors. Vector A, vector B. The angle between them is theta and we calculate the cosine
[310s-320s] of theta. The closer the two vectors are in their semantic meaning, the smaller the angles will be. Cosine similarity
[320s-330s] is calculated by taking the dot product between the two vectors A and B divided by
[330s-340s] the magnitude of the two vectors A and B. The output of the cosine similarity falls between
[340s-350s] negative 1 and 1 contrary to Euclidean distance. It is actually a standardized matrix that ensures
[350s-360s] the output never exceeds this bound. When the cosine similarity is equal to negative one,
[360s-370s] that indicates that the two vectors is pointing at the exact same direction.
[370s-380s] When the cosine similarity is equal to zero, that means the two vectors are pointing
[380s-390s] at 90 degree or perpendicular. In machine learning, when two vectors are perpendicular,
[390s-400s] it means that the features that they represent are completely independent of one another. If the cosine similarity is
[400s-410s] equal to negative one, that means that the two vectors are pointing towards the
[410s-420s] opposite direction. In machine learning, the vector dot product can be extended to matrix dot product. Matrix
[420s-430s] dot product is one of the most important operations in linear algebra. It is used in all kinds of neural networks ranging from simple
[430s-440s] vanilla neural net to state of the art transformer models. Now let's talk about training ML models in practice. Modern LLMs
[440s-450s] are trained on billions and billions of tokens and computations using all dimensions are impractical and inefficient. Instead, dimensionality
[450s-460s] reduction algorithms using linear algebra principles such as singular value decomposition or SVD allows us to perform matrix factorization,
[460s-470s] a process of breaking down a complex large matrix into smaller, more manageable and maintainable matrices such that they
[470s-480s] can be reconstructed back to form the large matrix. Using svd, we can break down one single large matrix A into three smaller
[480s-490s] matrices A. Let's think of A as a movie data set where it has rows
[490s-500s] and columns. So rows might represent user and the columns might represent the movies and the fields
[500s-510s] is populated with the user's rating of the movies. After vectorizing A into matrix into this large matrix,
[510s-520s] we can use SVD to break it down into three smaller matrices U sigma and V. Transposed
[520s-530s] U is the left singular matrix. It is a orthogonal matrix that represents features in the row space
[530s-540s] or the user space. It captures the behavior of the user. It captures the feature of the user. It gives us that representation from the original
[540s-550s] large matrix. Sigma is a diagonal matrix. That means every value
[550s-560s] is zero except for across the diagonal. In spd, Sigma is usually ordered such
[560s-570s] that the largest value comes in first, and it signifies the importance of each of the features V. Transposed
[570s-580s] is the matrix that represents features in the column space or the movie space. It captures information
[580s-590s] about the movie. Now we can reconstruct these three smaller matrices back to form the large matrix
[590s-600s] A. Using SVD algorithm, we can select and only retain the most informative features based on the singular values
[600s-610s] and disregard unhelpful information. SVD is not only elegant but extremely versatile because it's capable of performing matrix
[610s-620s] factorization on any matrices without restrictions on the shapes. Linear algebra provides the essential framework that allows machine learning system to
[620s-630s] operate. It transforms raw data into structured numerical representations, enables efficient computation through matrix operations,
[630s-640s] and supports large scale optimization through modern libraries such as PyTorch and TensorFlow. Methods like SVD
[640s-650s] further refine these representations by reducing dimensionality and highlighting the most informative structure within the data.
[650s-660s] Together, these principles illustrate why linear algebra remains foundational in machine learning. It converts data into mathematical
[660s-670s] form, computations into organized structure, and structure into actionable intelligence.
detected_topics: Linear algebra as the language of data
Vectorization of images, text, audio, video, and structured data
How linear algebra supports neural networks and LLMs
